---
title: "A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature"
author:
  - name: "Minh Ha-Duong\\thanks{Directeur de recherche, CIRED, CNRS. Correspondence: minh.ha-duong@cnrs.fr}"
    orcid: "0000-0001-9988-2100"
    affiliation:
      - name: "CIRED"
      - name: "CNRS"
date: "2026-03-26"
metadata-files: [data-paper-vars.yml]
bibliography: ../_shared/bibliography/main.bib
abstract: |
  This data paper presents a curated, multilingual corpus of
  {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance published between 1990
  and 2024. The dataset is assembled from
  {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources that combine academic databases,
  institutional repositories, and grey literature, enabling broader coverage
  than single-source bibliometric studies. A multilingual retrieval strategy
  based on an eight-language keyword taxonomy is used to capture relevant
  works across linguistic contexts, while a reproducible pipeline integrates
  deduplication, metadata harmonisation, and quality filtering. The resulting
  corpus includes a citation network derived from Crossref and OpenAlex, as
  well as pre-computed multilingual embeddings to support cross-lingual
  analysis.
keywords: |
  climate finance -- bibliometric corpus -- multilingual --
  sentence-transformer embeddings -- scientometrics -- history of economic thought
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
---

<!-- Target journal: Research Data Journal for the Humanities and Social Sciences (RDJ4HSS) -->
<!-- Format: Data Paper, max 2,500 words, diamond OA, IISG/Openjournals -->
<!-- Structure follows Petram & Kruizinga (2024) exemplar: Introduction, Method, Data, Concluding Remarks -->

- Related dataset: "A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature" with DOI [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130) in repository "Zenodo"

## 1. Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Climate finance---the financial flows directed at mitigating and adapting to climate change---has become one of the most politically salient objects in international economic governance, yet the scholarship that analyses it remains bibliographically fragmented [@roberts_weikmans2017; @michaelowa2007]. Academic publications are dispersed across databases with different coverage profiles. Institutional reports from the OECD, UNFCCC, World Bank, and Climate Policy Initiative are absent from standard academic databases. The literature is also multilingual: while English dominates, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German traditions contribute perspectives that monolingual corpora miss entirely.

Pioneering bibliometric work has begun to map this terrain. @care_weber2023 survey climate finance using Scopus; @shang_jin2023 analyse climate finance from Web of Science; @maria_etal2023 map green finance with network analysis. These studies rely on a single database each, search in English only, and do not publish reusable datasets. Our corpus contains most of what they mapped: replicating each study's published search query against OpenAlex and matching the retrieved works by DOI and year-constrained title, 89.3% of the population returned for the query of @care_weber2023 and 91.0% for that of @shang_jin2023 are present in this dataset. Coverage is 40.1% for the query of @maria_etal2023, which spans green finance at large, a broader field than this dataset covers by design.
<!-- Coverage numbers: revision-rdj26561/prior-mappings-overlap.csv, generated by
     revision-rdj26561/probe_prior_mappings_overlap.py (ticket 0289, run 2026-07-22). -->

Beyond coverage, the corpus can re-test what this literature reports; six of its results hold, each under one statistic:
<!-- Statistics: tab_lit_confirmations.csv and
     tab_semantic_robustness.csv (+ tab_sem6_assignments.csv, the per-work
     reproducibility artifact) in the shared tables dir, generated by scripts/analysis/
     compute_lit_confirmations.py, compute_sem6_assignments.py and
     compute_semantic_robustness.py (ticket 0310); the lit_* variables come
     from those artifacts via compute_vars.py — no hand-typed numbers. -->

- @diazrainey_etal2017 found that only 12 of 20,725 articles (0.06%) published in 21 leading finance journals between 1998 and mid-2015 concerned climate finance; our corpus confirms the near-absence and dates the turn, with the finance-journal share of corpus works rising from {{< meta lit_finshare_pre_pct >}}% before 2015 to {{< meta lit_finshare_post_pct >}}% after ($\chi^2$ = {{< meta lit_finshare_chi2 >}}, p {{< meta lit_finshare_p >}}).
- @kouwenberg_zheng2023 report exponential growth in climate finance publications, especially from 2015 onwards; we confirm the take-off, with a structural break in the trend of log annual corpus counts at 2015 (Chow F = {{< meta lit_growth_f >}}, p {{< meta lit_growth_p >}}) and output growing {{< meta lit_growth_post_pct >}}% a year since.
- @kouwenberg_zheng2023 also find the financial--economic author cluster "quite disconnected" from the rest of the field; we confirm on citation links, where edges between the public-finance pole (allocation, governance, loss-and-damage) and the market-finance pole (climate risk, green bonds, innovation finance) make up {{< meta lit_poles_cross_pct >}}% of edges against {{< meta lit_poles_cross_null_pct >}}% expected under degree-preserving rewiring (z = {{< meta lit_poles_z >}}, p {{< meta lit_poles_p >}}).
- The UNEP Adaptation Gap Report documents adaptation finance persistently lagging mitigation finance [@unep2023agr]; scholarly attention mirrors the gap, with {{< meta lit_adapt_n >}} corpus works mentioning adaptation but not mitigation against {{< meta lit_mitig_n >}} the other way round ({{< meta lit_adapt_share_pct >}}%, binomial p {{< meta lit_adapt_p >}}).
- The companion study [Ha-Duong, under review] partitions the field into six semantic clusters; k-means with k = 6 on the present corpus and its 1024-dimensional embeddings (against 384 dimensions in v1.0) recovers five of the six themes, with a land-use cluster emerging in place of the \$100bn/fund-flows theme, and the partition is stable across estimation variants (adjusted Rand index (ARI) $\geq$ {{< meta lit_sem6_ari >}} between raw, normalised, and restart variants over {{< meta lit_sem6_n >}} works).
- The two structurations of the corpus --- citation communities (@fig-global-map) and semantic clusters (@fig-sem-composition) --- do not correspond one for one: communities that share a vocabulary do not necessarily cite each other.

Our dataset addresses these limitations through {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources, a documented quality-filtering pipeline, and sentence-transformer embeddings that place all works in a shared semantic space regardless of language. Because "climate finance" is a contested category whose boundaries are themselves an object of study [@weikmans_roberts2019], we err on the side of inclusion and publish the full corpus with quality flags, letting users draw their own boundary. The dataset was constructed for a history-of-economic-thought study of how climate finance crystallised as a category of economic governance [Ha-Duong, under review], but its scope makes it reusable for topic modelling, citation network analysis, bibliometric mapping, and cross-lingual studies.

## 2. Method {#sec-method}

### 2.1 Sources {#sec-sources}

The corpus assembles academic and grey literature from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} sources with complementary coverage profiles (@tbl-sources). Four are automated or semi-automated (reproducible from the scripts and an internet connection); two require manual export from restricted platforms.

| Source | Automation | Coverage |
|--------|------------|----------|
| OpenAlex [@priem_etal2022] | Automated (free API) | Primary academic source (indexes Crossref, DOAJ, PubMed, and more): tiered keyword search |
| ISTEX | Automated (public API) | CNRS national text-mining platform (multi-publisher) |
| Grey literature | Hybrid (curated seed + World Bank API) | 16 curated reports [e.g., @buchner2013] + World Bank repository |
| Teaching canon | AI-assisted (scraping + LLM extraction) | Syllabus readings from university courses worldwide |
| bibCNRS | Hand-harvested (CNRS credentials) | CNRS portal aggregating non-English news & discourse (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) in FR, ZH, JA, DE |
| SciSpace | AI-collected, hand-exported | SciSpace systematic review tool exports |

: Sources, automation level, and coverage scope. {#tbl-sources}

The search strategy uses a four-tier keyword taxonomy reflecting the evolving vocabulary of climate finance. Tier 1 consists of core terms in eight languages (English, French, Chinese, Japanese, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Arabic). Tiers 2--4 are English-only: Tier 2 covers institutional and diplomatic vocabulary (e.g., "clean development mechanism," "green climate fund"), while Tiers 3 and 4 broaden the search to climate-adjacent terms with concept-group co-occurrence filters (2-of-4 and 3-of-4 respectively) to maintain precision. The taxonomy was informed by keyword mining of {{< meta corpus_core >}} core papers (cited $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} times) and is defined in a version-controlled YAML configuration file.

The corpus is multilingual by design. While English dominates ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}%), the strategy explicitly targeted French (*finance climat*, *finance climatique*), Chinese, Japanese, and German through ISTEX, bibCNRS, and multilingual OpenAlex queries. This deliberate multilingual scope, though it yields a modest non-English share, provides a foundation for cross-linguistic studies that monolingual corpora cannot support.

All API queries are bounded to publication years 1990--2024; the lower bound reflects the start of UNFCCC negotiations, before which "climate finance" had no institutional referent. Raw API responses are stored in an append-only pool as compressed JSONL files, preserving complete responses for future re-extraction without re-downloading. OpenAlex indexes economics working papers from RePEc, NBER, MPRA, and other repositories, so no separate harvest is needed. The two hand-harvested sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) together contribute 896 records to the raw pool before quality filtering (@tbl-quality); the bibCNRS aggregates (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) capture non-English public discourse absent from academic databases. Their inclusion is validated by multi-source overlap: 62% of SciSpace records and 9% of bibCNRS records also appear in automated sources, confirming retrieval consistency.

### 2.2 Data Structure {#sec-data-structure}

The merge pipeline combines records from all sources through two deduplication passes: DOI-based (normalised, lowercased), which removes {{< meta dedup_doi_removed >}} records, and title+year matching for records lacking DOIs, which removes a further {{< meta dedup_titleyear_removed >}}. When duplicates are found, the maximum citation count is retained and metadata follows a source-priority order. Boolean `from_*` columns (one per source) track which databases contributed each record, enabling provenance analysis. The `source_count` field records multi-source agreement: of the {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works, {{< meta corpus_multi_source >}} ({{< meta corpus_multi_source_pct >}}%) appear in multiple sources.

A six-flag filtering pipeline then evaluates every work. Three flags require no external data: (1) missing metadata (no title), (2) absent abstract combined with an irrelevant title (lacking domain-specific terms in any of the target languages), and (3) title blacklist matches (noise terms such as "blockchain" or "deep learning" without climate-finance context). Three flags depend on enrichment outputs: (4) citation isolation (pre-2020 works neither cited by nor citing any other corpus work), (5) semantic outlier detection (embedding distance from corpus centroid exceeding mean + 2 standard deviations), and (6) cross-encoder relevance scoring against the query "climate policy and financial mechanisms."

A separate annotation step detects near-duplicate content: coordinated multi-journal publications where the same text appears under different DOIs. Detection uses a two-pass approach (abstract prefix clustering followed by title clustering with abstract-similarity validation) and assigns a shared `near_duplicate_group` identifier. These records are kept, not removed: they are real publications.

The pipeline removes flagged works unless they meet one of four protection criteria: citation count $\geq$ 50, presence in 2+ sources, within-corpus citations, or appearance in teaching syllabi. The teaching canon was built by scraping university course syllabi worldwide and extracting bibliographic references through a combination of DOI regex matching and LLM-assisted extraction, followed by fuzzy deduplication and Crossref enrichment (details in the companion technical report). Every inclusion/exclusion decision is recorded in the audit trail.

### 2.3 Quality, Completeness, and Potential Biases {#sec-quality}

@tbl-quality summarises corpus composition and metadata completeness per source. The "Refined" column counts works with a `from_*` provenance flag after deduplication and quality filtering; a single work may appear in multiple rows if discovered by several sources. %non-EN is the share of non-English works, %DOI the share with a valid DOI, %Abstract the share with an abstract longer than 50 characters, and %Refs the share with at least one reference in the citation graph.

{{< include ../_shared/tables/tab_corpus_sources.md >}}

The merge pipeline reduces {{< meta corpus_raw >}} raw records to {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works ({{< meta corpus_removal_pct >}}% removal rate). Of the {{< meta filter_flagged >}} works flagged (counts are non-exclusive: a single work may trigger multiple flags), citation isolation ({{< meta filter_citation_isolated >}}) and cross-encoder relevance scoring ({{< meta filter_llm_irrelevant >}}) are the most common, followed by absent abstracts with irrelevant titles ({{< meta filter_no_abstract >}}), missing metadata ({{< meta filter_missing_metadata >}}), and title blacklist matches ({{< meta filter_title_blacklist >}}). Of these, {{< meta filter_protected >}} are retained by protection criteria, yielding {{< meta filter_net_removals >}} net removals; an additional 21 content-duplicates discovered during enrichment are also removed.

As @tbl-quality shows, OpenAlex contributes {{< meta openalex_pct >}}% of refined works; the five smaller sources increase the corpus's non-English, institutional, and pedagogical coverage. Citation graph verification on a sample of 300 links confirmed 99.0% against Crossref metadata (95% CI [97.1%, 99.7%]); the 1% unconfirmed are OpenAlex-sourced references absent from Crossref, not errors. A second check on 300 source DOIs found no Crossref references lost during harvesting. Restricting to links where both citing and cited works belong to the refined corpus yields {{< meta cite_refined_rows >}} corpus-internal citation pairs; {{< meta cite_refined_coverage_pct >}}% of corpus DOIs participate in at least one such pair, rising to {{< meta cite_coverage_core_pct >}}% for core papers (cited $\geq$ 50 times).

Verified links are one thing; complete reference lists are another. We recorded the number of references each work contributes to the citation graph. Among the {{< meta refs_doi_docs >}} DOI-bearing works, the median count is {{< meta refs_median >}} (mean {{< meta refs_mean >}}, 95th percentile {{< meta refs_p95 >}}, maximum {{< meta refs_max >}}). At one tail, {{< meta refs_zero_n >}} works ({{< meta refs_zero_share_pct >}}%) contribute no reference at all: for these, Crossref and OpenAlex index the work but not its reference list, a gap concentrated in books, book reviews, editorials, and grey literature. At the other tail, counts in the hundreds mark review articles and a few aberrant reference lists. Both tails matter for users: absence of outgoing links measures indexing, not scholarly isolation, and per-document reference counts are the screening variable to check before any citation-based analysis.

Relevance filtering (flag 6) uses a cross-encoder reranker (BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3, 568M parameters) for deterministic, reproducible scores. The query "climate policy and financial mechanisms" was selected from 20 candidates (AUC = 0.766 on weak labels); human validation on a blinded sample of 100 works yielded AUC = 0.818 and 81% accuracy. The chosen query privileges instrumental framing; works centred on climate justice without financial mechanisms vocabulary may score lower. These scores check the flagging pipeline; they are not ground truth, since what counts as "climate finance" is itself contested. The Zenodo deposit includes all calibration and validation tables.

{{< include ../_shared/tables/tab_languages.md >}}

Near-duplicate detection identified coordinated multi-journal publications --- notably the COP27 editorial [@atwoli_etal2022], published with identical or near-identical abstracts across 60+ journals under different DOIs. These are not data errors but a deliberate publication strategy; the `near_duplicate_group` column enables users to deduplicate by content if their analysis requires it.

Users should be aware of several potential biases. This corpus is strongest on the academic pool (Crossref, OpenAlex) and weakest on policy discourse and media. International organisations and think tanks are partially captured through the grey-literature source; news and public discourse are represented through bibCNRS. Non-English coverage remains limited (@tbl-languages): English accounts for {{< meta lang_english_pct >}}% of refined works despite targeting eight languages, reflecting structural biases in indexing systems. The enriched corpus contains works in 48 detected languages. Citation counts reflect OpenAlex data as of the collection date (2026-03-26) and favour older, English-language works. The teaching canon reflects a harvesting bias toward institutions with publicly accessible syllabi.

The grey-literature source is scoped to international institutions: the UNFCCC system, the OECD, the World Bank, and transnational think tanks such as the Climate Policy Initiative. It deliberately excludes documents from national institutions: government and parliamentary reports, economic analysis councils, central bank communications. The boundary follows the research object. The corpus was built to trace climate finance as a category of international economic governance, defined, negotiated, and accounted for in the UNFCCC and OECD arenas; national documents largely apply that category domestically rather than constitute it. The boundary is also a matter of tractability. National-institution documents form a universe with no common index or point of access, and each document class is large enough to be a corpus of its own: @campiglio_etal2025 assemble 35,487 speeches from 131 central banks to study climate-related communication for that single class. Sampling a few national sources would add noise without coverage; harvesting them systematically is a different project. The exclusion has a cost we acknowledge: national sources would broaden both institutional and multilingual coverage, since governments and central banks publish in their own languages. The provenance architecture (@sec-data-structure) accepts such a layer without reprocessing existing sources, and central bank communication, where curated open datasets now exist [@campiglio_etal2025], is the natural first extension.

### 2.4 OpenAlex limitations and pipeline responses {#sec-openalex-limits}

OpenAlex offers the widest coverage of any open scholarly database and indexes working papers, but its metadata quality is uneven [@priem_etal2022]. It supplies {{< meta openalex_pct >}}% of refined works, so @tbl-openalex-limits maps each documented flaw to the pipeline step that addresses it, or states that none does.

| OpenAlex limitation | Pipeline response |
|---------------------|-------------------|
| Query false positives | Six-flag filter (@sec-data-structure); cross-encoder relevance scoring, human-validated at AUC = 0.818 |
| Duplicate records | Two deduplication passes at merge (DOI-based; title+year); `near_duplicate_group` annotation for same-content works under distinct DOIs |
| Missing abstracts | Enrichment reconstructs abstracts from OpenAlex inverted indices and ISTEX fulltext; residual absences trigger flag 2 |
| Incorrect citation assignments | Audit of 300 sampled links against Crossref: 99.0% confirmed (@sec-quality) |
| Empty or aberrant reference lists | Per-document reference counts recorded and reported (@sec-quality); GROBID parses the unstructured reference strings Crossref and OpenAlex leave unresolved (@sec-data) |
| Contaminated abstract fields | Boilerplate detection excludes stub abstracts from embeddings; see below for the effect on @fig-bars |
| Missing full-text | Not mitigated: we do not hold the full texts of the corpus's academic works, so the citation graph relies on indexed metadata and deposited reference strings; no-DOI works (28%) are undercounted |

: Documented OpenAlex limitations and the pipeline steps that address them. {#tbl-openalex-limits}

The abstract field needs the most care. OpenAlex sometimes stores boilerplate (paywall notices, repository metadata, the title repeated) or, for some journals and publishers, the full text or opening sections of the article instead of the abstract. A boilerplate detector (`is_boilerplate_abstract` in the deposited code) drops stub abstracts before embedding, so affected works are embedded from title and keywords only. In @fig-bars, which shades works matching "climate finance" in title or abstract, an over-long abstract field can add a work to the shaded subset only when the phrase appears in the body text but not the abstract; the bias is upward and confined to the shading, since corpus membership does not depend on abstract length.

Deduplication is the residual weak point. A working paper and its published version rarely share either merge key: their DOIs differ or are absent, and the year — sometimes the title — changes at publication, so such pairs escape both passes. An audit of the refined corpus bounds the damage. Works sharing an exact normalised title and first-author surname but published up to five years apart form {{< meta dedup_fn_pairs >}} candidate pairs ({{< meta dedup_fn_pairs_pct >}}% of works); inspection attributes about half to coordinated multi-journal editorials already flagged in `near_duplicate_group`, the other half to genuine version pairs of the working-paper kind. Relaxing the title match to fuzzy overlap raises the upper bound to {{< meta dedup_fn_upper_docs >}} works ({{< meta dedup_fn_upper_pct >}}%), but this looser tier is dominated by report series and successive editions that are distinct works. The passes also over-merge occasionally: {{< meta dedup_fp_doi_collision_groups >}} DOI groups join records with unrelated titles (OpenAlex assigns one DOI to several works), and the title+year key degrades when the year is empty, merging {{< meta dedup_fp_empty_year_docs >}} generically titled works into {{< meta dedup_fp_empty_year_groups >}} groups. Matching on authors, a natural refinement, would target the version pairs; author metadata is itself noisy across sources (given and family names swap order), so author-normalised deduplication is left to a future release. The audit script and its output table (`tab_dedup_error_estimates.csv`) ship with the pipeline code.

The corpus could be complemented with regional databases whose venues OpenAlex covers thinly: SciELO for Latin American journals, where Brazilian scholarship on green and climate finance is active, or Garuda for Indonesian journals. Neither was harvested here; the provenance architecture (@sec-data-structure) accepts such additions without reprocessing existing sources.

## 3. Data {#sec-data}

The Zenodo deposit (DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130), temporal coverage 1990--2024) is a reproducibility archive in three parts: the pipeline source code (`code/`: scripts, configuration, and DVC definitions), the raw data inputs (`data/inputs/`: the six per-source catalogs as harvested, before merge and deduplication), and the final data products of this paper (`data/products/`: the v1.1 corpus files described below). All CSV files use UTF-8 encoding with standard comma-separated format; identifiers are opaque string keys.

**climate_finance_corpus.csv** ({{< meta corpus_raw >}} rows). The complete, unfiltered corpus. Each row is one deduplicated work; the columns follow four logical groups — record identity, bibliographic metadata, provenance flags, and curation metadata — and @tbl-variables describes every column. A codebook shipped with the deposit (`codebook.md`) adds, for each column, the allowed values and the share of missing values measured on the deposited file. The quality flags are the audit trail: users reproduce our refined subset ({{< meta corpus_total >}} works) with `df[~df['is_flagged'] | df['is_protected']]`. The `near_duplicate_group` column identifies coordinated multi-journal publications sharing near-identical content, and the version-tracking flag `in_v1` marks works present in the v1.0 submission corpus. An enrichment step reconstructs missing abstracts from OpenAlex inverted indices and ISTEX fulltext (TEI XML), replacing boilerplate stubs where possible. Abstracts are not included in the deposited file due to publisher redistribution restrictions; they can be retrieved programmatically via the DOI or OpenAlex identifier. @fig-bars shows the temporal distribution.

{{< include ../_shared/tables/tab_variables.md >}}

**embeddings.npz.** Compressed NumPy archive (keys: `vectors` as float32 array, `keys` as string array of identifiers) containing L2-normalised (unit-length) {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional vectors for {{< meta corpus_with_embeddings >}} of the {{< meta corpus_raw >}} works, computed by the multilingual sentence-transformer *BAAI/bge-m3*. Works without a title are excluded; works with missing or boilerplate abstracts (paywall stubs, repository metadata) are embedded from title and keywords only. Row *i* of `vectors` corresponds to `keys[i]`. Flagged works are included to support alternative filtering strategies. Each vector encodes the concatenation of title, abstract (when available and non-boilerplate), and keywords within the model's 8192-token context window (no truncation for typical abstracts). The model maps texts in English, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German into a shared semantic space, which is designed to group works by topic rather than by language. These vectors serve as direct input for BERTopic, top2vec, or other embedding-based topic models, and support cosine-similarity search.

**citations.csv.** Citation network ({{< meta cite_total_rows >}} rows). Columns: `source_doi`, `ref_doi` (both normalised, lowercased DOIs). Each row records a (citing, cited) pair where the citing work belongs to the corpus; the cited work may or may not. For corpus-internal citations only, join on the corpus identifiers. The citation network operates on DOI pairs: works without DOIs cannot be matched as citing sources, and references without DOIs are retained but cannot be deduplicated. Since 28% of records lack DOIs, citation coverage is incomplete for early and grey-literature works. The pipeline applies GROBID at the reference-string level: `corpus_parse_citations_grobid.py` (in the deposited code) parses the free-text citation strings that Crossref and OpenAlex return unresolved, recovering title, first author, and year, which a fuzzy-matching step then resolves against corpus works. Full-text reference extraction — parsing the PDF of every corpus document — is not performed, because we do not hold the full texts of the academic works; GROBID therefore operates on deposited reference strings across the whole corpus, not on PDFs.

**Source catalogs.** Per-source catalogs (`openalex_works.csv`, `istex_works.csv`, etc.), shipped under `data/inputs/`, enable provenance tracking back to individual databases before merge and deduplication. The corpus does not include a dedicated Open Access or fulltext URL column; users can recover OA links via the OpenAlex API using the `source_id` field, or construct ISTEX fulltext URLs from ISTEX identifiers in the source catalogs.

![Annual publication volume in the corpus. The search window is 1990--2024; the earliest works date from 1992.](../_shared/figures/fig_bars.png){#fig-bars width=100%}

Citation coverage --- the share of corpus works that appear as a citing source in the citation network --- is unevenly distributed over time: approximately 27% for works published before 2007 versus 47% for post-2015 works. This gap reflects two compounding factors: early and grey-literature works are less likely to carry DOIs or to have their reference lists indexed by Crossref and OpenAlex, and the field itself was smaller before 2007. Users conducting temporal citation-network analyses should account for this systematic undercount of early-period connectivity.

@fig-global-map shows what the citation network resolves at full-corpus scale: Louvain community detection on the intra-corpus citation graph yields {{< meta gm_communities >}} major communities covering {{< meta gm_coverage_pct >}}% of the {{< meta gm_n_connected >}} connected works. The communities correspond to recognisable research programmes, from carbon markets to green bonds; the companion paper [Ha-Duong, under review] develops this community-level analysis.

![Global map of climate finance scholarship: direct-citation communities among corpus documents. The field is bipolar --- public climate finance (international governance, CDM and carbon markets, climate science) on one side, market-based finance (green bonds and sustainable finance, financial economics of climate risk) on the other --- with North--South allocation at the centre. Reading guide: each circle is one Louvain community of corpus works; circle area is proportional to community membership, edge width to the number of citation links between communities. Method: undirected graph of direct citations between corpus documents (DOI-matched), Louvain detection with fixed seed, modularity {{< meta gm_modularity >}}; the {{< meta gm_communities >}} communities holding at least 2% of nodes each cover {{< meta gm_coverage_pct >}}% of the {{< meta gm_n_connected >}} connected works. Layout: force-directed embedding of the community meta-graph (distances are indicative only); names and colours follow a registry shared across all corpus network figures.](../_shared/figures/fig_global_map_direct.png){#fig-global-map width=100%}

![Thematic composition shifts across the three periods: Kyoto-era clusters contract while green bonds and Paris-era vocabularies expand. Six k-means clusters (k = 6, fixed seed) of the multilingual embeddings, recomputed on the current corpus from the companion study's appendix figure; each panel gives the cluster's share of works published Before (1990--2006), during Crystallisation (2007--2014), and during Disputes (2015--2024), with its top TF-IDF terms as subtitle. Works whose abstract field holds repository boilerplate (@sec-openalex-limits) are excluded from the clustering input.](../_shared/figures/fig_sem_composition.png){#fig-sem-composition width=90%}

The dataset is released under CC BY 4.0. The four automated or semi-automated sources can be re-harvested with an internet connection; the two restricted sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) have their raw exports included in the Zenodo deposit.

## 4. Concluding Remarks {#sec-concluding}

This corpus assembles climate finance scholarship into a single, cross-lingual bibliographic object. Its {{< meta corpus_sources >}}-source design pairs the breadth of OpenAlex with coverage from French archives (ISTEX), non-English discourse (bibCNRS), institutional reports (grey literature), pedagogical use (teaching canon), and AI-assisted discovery (SciSpace).

Can such a corpus serve the history of economic thought? The companion paper [Ha-Duong, under review] is the proof of use. It argues that climate finance crystallised as a category of economic governance around 2009, and it assigns the corpus a deliberately modest epistemic role: the quantitative evidence corroborates a history built from documents, actors, and dates --- it neither discovers nor falsifies it. Three method families carry that corroboration, and the citation graph is only one. Semantic methods read the embeddings: structural-break detection on the corpus's yearly embedding distributions, blind to the negotiation calendar, finds its main rupture at 2007. Lexical methods track the vocabulary itself, dating when the field's terms entered and left use. And plain descriptive statistics do honest work: annual counts, source overlaps, co-citation tallies.

The referee's caution deserves a candid reply: any focused research question will demand task-specific cleaning beyond the generic filtering documented here. The corpus is reusable infrastructure, not a finished analysis, and our own use shows both the limit and what survives it. Community-separation tests fail on the pre-2007 window --- fewer than 50 works per year is too thin a base for any statistic to escape its null. Yet qualitative results stand on simple counts: @negishi1960, the welfare-weights framework one would expect to anchor burden-sharing debates, is absent from the corpus's pre-2007 canon, while Scott Barrett's treaty-participation economics [@barrett2003participation] is co-cited with the carbon-pricing literature rather than with institutionalist scholarship. Where inference runs out, description still speaks.

Beyond this programme, the dataset supports directions we have not pursued. The provenance and author metadata allow networks of institutions and co-authors, mapping the field's key actors across academic and institutional worlds. The embeddings feed topic models and content classification to document how the literature's focus shifted. The multilingual design permits comparing how the category travelled across languages.

The provenance architecture makes it straightforward to add new sources or extend the temporal window. A future iteration would benefit from OpenAlex's bulk download rather than keyword-based API queries, reducing dependence on vocabulary choices made at harvest time. By making its boundaries explicit rather than implicit, the corpus turns the definition of "climate finance" from a hidden assumption into an object of analysis.

## Data and Code Availability {.unnumbered}

The corpus data files and pipeline source code are deposited in Zenodo under CC BY 4.0 at [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130). Key software dependencies: Python $\geq$ 3.10, pandas, sentence-transformers, and DVC. Suggested citation:

> Ha-Duong, M. (2026). A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990–2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature [Data set]. Zenodo. [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130)

## References {.unnumbered}
